mn10:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Kurus, in a Kuru town named Kammāsadhamma.
mn10:10.0: 1.4. Section on Contemplation of the Body: Attention to Repulsiveness
mn10:10.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, from the soles of the feet upward, from the crown of the head downward, bounded by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
mn10:10.2: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, membranes, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, spittle, snot, joint-fluid, and urine.’
mn10:10.3: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a bag open at both ends might be full of various kinds of grain, that is: hill rice, paddy rice, mung beans, peas, sesame, and husked rice; and a sharp-eyed person, having opened it, would review it thus: ‘These are hill rice, these are paddy rice, these are mung beans, these are peas, these are sesame, these are husked rice.’
mn10:10.4: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, from the soles of the feet upward, from the crown of the head downward, bounded by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
mn10:10.5: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs … urine.’
mn10:11.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body …
mn10:11.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:11.3: The section on attention to repulsiveness is finished.
mn10:12.0: 1.5. Section on Contemplation of the Body: Attention to the Elements
mn10:12.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, however it is placed, however it is positioned, in terms of elements:
mn10:12.2: ‘In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.’
mn10:12.3: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a skilled butcher or a butcher’s apprentice, having killed a cow, might sit at a crossroads having divided it up into portions.
mn10:12.4: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, however it is placed, however it is positioned, in terms of elements:
mn10:12.5: ‘In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.’
mn10:13.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body …
mn10:13.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:13.3: The section on attention to the elements is finished.
mn10:14.0: 1.6. Section on Contemplation of the Body: The Nine Charnel-Ground Contemplations
mn10:14.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, suppose a *bhikkhu* were to see a corpse thrown aside in a charnel ground, dead for one day, two days, or three days, bloated, livid, and oozing with pus.
mn10:14.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn10:14.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will be like that, and has not gone beyond that.’
mn10:15.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body …
mn10:15.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:16.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, suppose a *bhikkhu* were to see a corpse thrown aside in a charnel ground being eaten by crows, or being eaten by hawks, or being eaten by vultures, or being eaten by dogs, or being eaten by jackals, or by various kinds of living creatures.
mn10:16.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn10:16.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will be like that, and has not gone beyond that.’
mn10:17.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body …
mn10:17.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:18-23.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, suppose a *bhikkhu* were to see a corpse thrown aside in a charnel ground, a skeleton with flesh and blood, held together with sinews …
mn10:18-23.2: A skeleton without flesh, smeared with blood, held together with sinews …
mn10:18-23.3: A skeleton without flesh and blood, held together with sinews …
mn10:2.1: “This is the direct path, *bhikkhus*, for the purification of beings, for the going beyond sorrow and lamentation, for the ending of *dukkha* and distress, for the attainment of the right way, for the realization of *nibbāna*—that is, the four *satipaṭṭhānas*.
mn10:24.1: Bones disconnected, scattered in all directions: here a hand bone, there a foot bone, here an ankle bone, there a shin bone, here a thigh bone, there a hip bone, here a rib bone, there a spine bone, here a shoulder bone, there a neck bone, here a jaw bone, there a tooth, here a skull.
mn10:24.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn10:24.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will be like that, and has not gone beyond that.’
mn10:25.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body …
mn10:25.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:26-28.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, suppose a *bhikkhu* were to see a corpse thrown aside in a charnel ground, bones white in color, like the color of shells …
mn10:29.1: Bones heaped together, more than a year old …
mn10:3.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having put away desire and distress regarding the world;
mn10:3.3: he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having put away desire and distress regarding the world;
mn10:3.4: he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*], ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having put away desire and distress regarding the world;
mn10:3.5: he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having put away desire and distress regarding the world.
mn10:30.1: Bones rotten and crumbled to powder.
mn10:30.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn10:30.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will be like that, and has not gone beyond that.’
mn10:31.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally;
mn10:31.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in the body.
mn10:31.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There is a body,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:31.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:31.5: The section on the nine charnel-ground contemplations is finished.
mn10:31.6: The fourteen contemplations of the body are finished.
mn10:32.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*?
mn10:32.10: When feeling an unworldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he knows: ‘I feel an unworldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, when feeling a pleasant *vedanā*, a *bhikkhu* knows: ‘I feel a pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.3: When feeling a painful *vedanā*, he knows: ‘I feel a painful *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.4: When feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he knows: ‘I feel a neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.5: When feeling a worldly pleasant *vedanā*, he knows: ‘I feel a worldly pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.6: When feeling an unworldly pleasant *vedanā*, he knows: ‘I feel an unworldly pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.7: When feeling a worldly painful *vedanā*, he knows: ‘I feel a worldly painful *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.8: When feeling an unworldly painful *vedanā*, he knows: ‘I feel an unworldly painful *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.9: When feeling a worldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he knows: ‘I feel a worldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn10:33.1: So he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* internally, or he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* externally, or he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* both internally and externally;
mn10:33.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *vedanā*; or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *vedanā*; or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in *vedanā*.
mn10:33.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There is *vedanā*,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:33.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*.
mn10:34.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*]?
mn10:34.10: He knows an expansive mind [*citta*] as ‘an expansive mind [*citta*].’
mn10:34.11: He knows a non-expansive mind [*citta*] as ‘a non-expansive mind [*citta*].’
mn10:34.12: He knows a surpassable mind [*citta*] as ‘a surpassable mind [*citta*].’
mn10:34.13: He knows an unsurpassable mind [*citta*] as ‘an unsurpassable mind [*citta*].’
mn10:34.14: He knows a mind [*citta*] endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*] as ‘a mind [*citta*] endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*].’
mn10:34.15: He knows a mind [*citta*] not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*] as ‘a mind [*citta*] not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*].’
mn10:34.16: He knows a liberated mind [*citta*] as ‘a liberated mind [*citta*].’
mn10:34.17: He knows an unliberated mind [*citta*] as ‘an unliberated mind [*citta*].’
mn10:34.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* knows a mind [*citta*] with lust as ‘a mind [*citta*] with lust.’
mn10:34.3: He knows a mind [*citta*] free of lust as ‘a mind [*citta*] free of lust.’
mn10:34.4: He knows a mind [*citta*] with aversion as ‘a mind [*citta*] with aversion.’
mn10:34.5: He knows a mind [*citta*] free of aversion as ‘a mind [*citta*] free of aversion.’
mn10:34.6: He knows a mind [*citta*] with delusion as ‘a mind [*citta*] with delusion.’
mn10:34.7: He knows a mind [*citta*] free of delusion as ‘a mind [*citta*] free of delusion.’
mn10:34.8: He knows a contracted mind [*citta*] as ‘a contracted mind [*citta*].’
mn10:34.9: He knows a distracted mind [*citta*] as ‘a distracted mind [*citta*].’
mn10:35.1: So he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*] internally, or he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*] externally, or he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*] both internally and externally;
mn10:35.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in mind [*citta*], or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in mind [*citta*], or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in mind [*citta*].
mn10:35.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There is mind [*citta*],’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:35.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*].
mn10:36.0.2: 4.1. Section on Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Hindrances
mn10:36.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*?
mn10:36.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five hindrances.
mn10:36.3: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five hindrances?
mn10:36.4: Here, *bhikkhus*, when sensual desire is present within, a *bhikkhu* knows: ‘There is sensual desire within me’; or when sensual desire is not present within, he knows: ‘There is no sensual desire within me.’ And he knows how unarisen sensual desire comes to arise, and he knows how arisen sensual desire comes to be abandoned, and he knows how abandoned sensual desire does not arise again in the future.
mn10:36.5: When ill will is present within, he knows: ‘There is ill will within me’; or when ill will is not present within, he knows: ‘There is no ill will within me.’ And he knows how unarisen ill will comes to arise, and he knows how arisen ill will comes to be abandoned, and he knows how abandoned ill will does not arise again in the future.
mn10:36.6: When dullness and drowsiness are present within, he knows: ‘There are dullness and drowsiness within me’; or when dullness and drowsiness are not present within, he knows: ‘There are no dullness and drowsiness within me.’ And he knows how unarisen dullness and drowsiness come to arise, and he knows how arisen dullness and drowsiness come to be abandoned, and he knows how abandoned dullness and drowsiness do not arise again in the future.
mn10:36.7: When restlessness and remorse are present within, he knows: ‘There are restlessness and remorse within me’; or when restlessness and remorse are not present within, he knows: ‘There are no restlessness and remorse within me.’ And he knows how unarisen restlessness and remorse come to arise, and he knows how arisen restlessness and remorse come to be abandoned, and he knows how abandoned restlessness and remorse do not arise again in the future.
mn10:36.8: When doubt is present within, he knows: ‘There is doubt within me’; or when doubt is not present within, he knows: ‘There is no doubt within me.’ And he knows how unarisen doubt comes to arise, and he knows how arisen doubt comes to be abandoned, and he knows how abandoned doubt does not arise again in the future.
mn10:37.1: So he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:37.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:37.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There are *dhammas*,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:37.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five hindrances.
mn10:37.5: The section on the hindrances is finished.
mn10:38.0: 4.2. Section on Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Aggregates
mn10:38.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*].
mn10:38.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*]?
mn10:38.4: ‘This is *rūpa*, this is the arising of *rūpa*, this is the passing away of *rūpa*;
mn10:38.5: this is *vedanā*, this is the arising of *vedanā*, this is the passing away of *vedanā*;
mn10:38.6: this is *saññā*, this is the arising of *saññā*, this is the passing away of *saññā*;
mn10:38.7: these are *saṅkhāras*, this is the arising of *saṅkhāras*, this is the passing away of *saṅkhāras*;
mn10:38.8: this is consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], this is the arising of consciousness [*viññāṇassa*], this is the passing away of consciousness [*viññāṇassa*].’
mn10:39.1: So he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:39.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:39.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There are *dhammas*,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:39.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*].
mn10:39.5: The section on the aggregates is finished.
mn10:4.0.2: 1.1. Section on Contemplation of the Body: Breathing In and Out
mn10:4.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating body in body?
mn10:4.10: he trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out’;
mn10:4.11: he trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, sits down, having folded his legs crosswise, set his body straight, and established *sati* to the fore.
mn10:4.3: With *sati* he breathes in; with *sati* he breathes out.
mn10:4.4: Breathing in long, he knows: ‘I breathe in long’; breathing out long, he knows: ‘I breathe out long’;
mn10:4.5: breathing in short, he knows: ‘I breathe in short’; breathing out short, he knows: ‘I breathe out short.’
mn10:4.6: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.7: He trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.8: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a skilled turner or a turner’s apprentice, when making a long turn, knows: ‘I am making a long turn,’ or when making a short turn, knows: ‘I am making a short turn’;
mn10:4.9: so too, *bhikkhus*, when a *bhikkhu* breathes in long, he knows: ‘I breathe in long’; when he breathes out long, he knows: ‘I breathe out long’; when he breathes in short, he knows: ‘I breathe in short’; when he breathes out short, he knows: ‘I breathe out short’;
mn10:40.0: 4.3. Section on Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Dimensions
mn10:40.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatanesu*].
mn10:40.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatanesu*]?
mn10:40.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, he knows the eye, and he knows forms, and he knows the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he knows how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, and he knows how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and he knows how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.4: He knows the ear, and he knows sounds, and he knows the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he knows how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, and he knows how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and he knows how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.5: He knows the nose, and he knows odors, and he knows the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he knows how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, and he knows how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and he knows how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.6: He knows the tongue, and he knows tastes, and he knows the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he knows how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, and he knows how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and he knows how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.7: He knows the body, and he knows tangibles, and he knows the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he knows how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, and he knows how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and he knows how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.8: He knows the mind, and he knows *dhammas*, and he knows the fetter that arises dependent on both; and he knows how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, and he knows how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and he knows how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:41.1: So he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:41.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:41.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There are *dhammas*,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:41.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatanesu*].
mn10:41.5: The section on the dimensions is finished.
mn10:42.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the seven *bojjhaṅgas*.
mn10:42.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the seven *bojjhaṅgas*?
mn10:42.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, when the *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within, a *bhikkhu* knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within me’; or when the *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within me.’ And he knows how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to arise, and he knows how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.4: When the *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within me’; or when the *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within me.’ And he knows how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to arise, and he knows how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.5: When the *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within me’; or when the *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within me.’ And he knows how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to arise, and he knows how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.6: When the *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within me’; or when the *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within me.’ And he knows how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to arise, and he knows how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.7: When the *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within me’; or when the *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within me.’ And he knows how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to arise, and he knows how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.8: When the *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within me’; or when the *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within me.’ And he knows how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to arise, and he knows how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.9: When the *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is present within me’; or when the *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within, he knows: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ*] is not present within me.’ And he knows how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to arise, and he knows how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:43.1: So he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:43.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:43.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There are *dhammas*,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:43.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the seven *bojjhaṅgas*.
mn10:43.5: The section on the *bojjhaṅgas* is finished.
mn10:44.0: 4.5. Section on Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Truths
mn10:44.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the four noble truths.
mn10:44.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the four noble truths?
mn10:44.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* understands as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn10:45.1: So he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally;
mn10:45.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:45.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There are *dhammas*,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:45.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* in the four noble truths.
mn10:45.5: The section on the truths is finished.
mn10:46.1: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven years, one of two fruits could be expected for him:
mn10:46.11: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven months, one of two fruits could be expected for him:
mn10:46.12: either final knowledge in this very life, or, if there is a remainder, non-return.
mn10:46.14: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for six months …
mn10:46.2: either final knowledge in this very life, or, if there is a remainder, non-return.
mn10:46.22: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven days, one of two fruits could be expected for him:
mn10:46.23: either final knowledge in this very life, or, if there is a remainder, non-return.
mn10:46.4: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for six years …
mn10:47.1: ‘This is the direct path, *bhikkhus*, for the purification of beings, for the going beyond sorrow and lamentation, for the ending of *dukkha* and distress, for the attainment of the right way, for the realization of *nibbāna*—that is, the four *satipaṭṭhānas*.’
mn10:47.2: And it was with reference to this that this was said.”
mn10:47.4: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn10:47.6: The Root Exposition Chapter, the first, is finished.
mn10:48.2: Terror, Without Blemish, One Might Wish, the Simile of the Cloth;
mn10:48.4: the excellent chapter, unequaled, very well complete.
mn10:5.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally;
mn10:5.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in the body.
mn10:5.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There is a body,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:5.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:5.5: The section on breathing in and out is finished.
mn10:6.0: 1.2. Section on Contemplation of the Body: Postures
mn10:6.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, when a *bhikkhu* is walking, he knows: ‘I am walking’; when standing, he knows: ‘I am standing’; when sitting, he knows: ‘I am sitting’; when lying down, he knows: ‘I am lying down.’
mn10:6.2: Or however his body is positioned, he knows it as such.
mn10:7.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally;
mn10:7.2: or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising and vanishing in the body.
mn10:7.3: Or else his *sati* is established: ‘There is a body,’ just to the extent of knowledge and *sati*. And he dwells independent, not clinging [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn10:7.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:7.5: The section on postures is finished.
mn10:8.0: 1.3. Section on Contemplation of the Body: Full Awareness
mn10:8.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when going forward and returning; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when looking ahead and looking away; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when bending and stretching; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in wearing the outer robe and carrying the bowl and robes; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in defecating and urinating; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when walking, standing, sitting, falling asleep, waking up, speaking, and remaining silent.
mn10:9.1: So he dwells contemplating body in body …
mn10:9.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:9.3: The section on full awareness is finished.
